free_text: Extension of the energy range of experimental
activation cross-sections data of deuteron induced
nuclear reactions on indium up to 50 MeV
Indium metal foils (commercial).
The 50 micron thick In foils were placed onto a
50 micron Al backing and wrapped with 6 micron Al for
protection.
Three stacks were bombarded with deuterons.
Beam energies: 50 MeV, irradiation time: 1 h
beam intensities: 32 nA.
The stack was mounted in a Faraday-cup like target
holder equipped with a suppressor of secondary
electrons.
The activities of the produced radionuclides were
measured nondestructively, without physical separation
of target and covering foils and without chemical
separation.
The source-detector distance was kept large enough to
minimize dead time and pile-up effects and possible
influence of the non point-like geometry of the
samples compared to calibration standards.
The energy degradation as a function of depth in the
stack was calculated from the input energy and target
thicknesses.
Al and Ti foils.
Chapter 4
Decay data were taken from NuDat2.6 database.
The monitoring method allows estimating the
uncertainty on the primary beam energy (+-0.3 MeV)
which is continuously rising in each foil through the
stack and attains +-0.7 MeV in the last In foil.
The following experimental uncertainties
were considered:
number of target nuclei including
non-uniformity (5%)
incident particle flux (7%),
peak area including statistical 0.1-20%
detector efficiency (5%) and
gamma-ray abundance and branching rate
data (3%).
Typical total uncertainty is between 10-15%.
Possible additional uncertainties due to non-linear
effects (like half-life and waiting time) were not
accounted for.
The experimental results were compared to the
curves calculated with the ALICE-D and EMPIRE-D
theoretical model codes and to the curves given in the
EAF-2007 and TENDL-2009 and TENDL-2014 databases.
Integral yields were calculated from the
fitted experimental cross sections.
